text: The surgeon performs a RTAP ventral hernia repair on an epigastric hernia with a small umbilical component. Three 8mm ports are placed on the left side, and the surgeon dissects parallel to the preperitoneal fat pad using blunt dissection. The flap is extended inferiorly, crossing the midline below the hernia defect. Scissor dissection is used as the fat adheres more to the posterior sheath near the hernia. The epigastric hernia defect is bilobed with a bridge of fascia and a large wad of preperitoneal fat and phosphor ligaments within the defect, which are reduced.

The surgeon performs a robotic transabdominal preperitoneal (RTAP) repair for a ventral hernia, starting with the creation of a flap on the left side, just outside the preperitoneal fat pad. Three eight-millimeter ports are used on the left, and the dissection follows parallel to the preperitoneal fat pad, utilizing blunt dissection. The surgeon dissects superiorly and inferiorly around the hernia defect, keeping the preperitoneal fat down and the white posterior sheath up to avoid perforations. The flap is extended inferiorly, crossing the midline below the hernia defect. As the dissection approaches the epigastric hernia, more adherent fat requires scissor dissection. The surgeon dissects above and below the hernia defect, sometimes reaching behind it, before starting the reduction.

The surgeon reduces the primary hernia, revealing a bilobed defect with a bridge of fascia and a large mass of pre-peritoneal fat and phosphor ligaments within the hernia sac. The dissection continues to the contralateral side, closely following the posterior sheath. The surgeon uncovers a small umbilical hernia defect with incarcerated fat and divides the umbilical ligaments, creating space for mesh placement.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the contralateral side, closely following the posterior sheath within the preperitoneal fat pad. The dissection involves a combination of blunt and sharp techniques, using cautery to carefully expand the pocket for mesh placement. The surgeon uncovers a small hernia defect near the umbilicus with incarcerated fat and divides the umbilical ligaments. The dissection progresses laterally to ensure adequate space for a fifteen by fifteen mesh, while avoiding creating holes in the thin tissue planes.

The surgeon continues lateral dissection towards the contralateral side using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection with cautery. Care is taken to avoid creating holes in the thin tissue planes, as the dissection progresses to create a wide pocket for mesh placement.

The surgeon measures the pocket size for mesh placement, ensuring the peritoneal flap extends across the viscera and past the midline. A heavyweight 15x15 mesh is selected due to the patient's body habitus. The surgeon closes the hernia defect using sutures, reducing pressure to about ten, and confirms the closure without penetrating the skin. The small umbilical hernia defect is also closed with the same suture, despite being covered by the large mesh.

The surgeon measures the pocket size for mesh placement, ensuring the peritoneal flap extends across the viscera and past the midline. A heavyweight 15x15 mesh is selected due to the patient's body habitus. The surgeon closes the hernia defect using sutures, confirming the closure does not penetrate the skin, and reduces pressure to facilitate closure. The small umbilical hernia defect is also closed with the same suture, providing coverage with the large mesh.

The surgeon places a mesh centered over the main hernia defect using a 3-0 silk suture, securing it with tacking sutures at the corners to prevent rolling. The mesh is positioned on the right side, both superiorly and inferiorly, and on the ipsilateral side. The flap is closed over the mesh, utilizing the laxity of the flap to cover it completely. The surgeon sutures through the fat pad for added strength, addressing a small hole with a V-lock suture. The final view shows the inverted sac, marking the end of the procedure.

The surgeon places the mesh, using a 3-0 silk suture to secure it at the center by passing through the mesh and the main hernia defect. The mesh is then anchored with tacking sutures at the corners to prevent rolling, including on the ipsilateral side. The flap is closed over the mesh, with sutures passing through the fat pad to enhance strength and prevent tearing.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field, noting the placement of the mesh and the closure of a small hole using a V-lock suture. The inverted sac is visible, marking the completion of the procedure.
